events.title: SpaceX Incorporated
events.detail: Elon Musk incorporated Space Exploration Technologies Corporation in Delaware on March 14, 2002, investing $100M from his PayPal proceeds. The company's stated goal was to dramatically reduce launch costs and ultimately enable human colonization of Mars. The company operationally opened in El Segundo, California in May 2002.

events.title: Tom Mueller and Chris Thompson — First Employees
events.detail: Propulsion engineer Tom Mueller and engineer Chris Thompson became SpaceX's first and second employees. Mueller's work on the Merlin engine would prove foundational to every SpaceX launch vehicle. The company initially operated from a 3,000 sq ft warehouse in El Segundo, CA before moving to Hawthorne.

events.title: SpaceX Acquires Hawthorne Factory
events.detail: SpaceX acquired a 550,000 sq ft manufacturing facility in Hawthorne, California — a former Boeing 747 fuselage factory. The building became SpaceX's primary headquarters and manufacturing campus, housing design, production, and mission control under one roof, enabling the vertical integration strategy central to cost reduction.

events.title: McGregor, Texas Engine Test Facility Opens
events.detail: SpaceX leased the former Bluebonnet Ordnance Plant site in McGregor, Texas, converting it into a rocket engine testing facility. Every Merlin, Draco, and later Raptor engine produced by SpaceX is hot-fire tested here before flight. The facility enabled rapid engine iteration essential to SpaceX's development philosophy.

events.title: Merlin 1A Engine First Test Fire
events.detail: SpaceX completed initial full-duration test fires of the Merlin 1A engine at McGregor. The regeneratively cooled engine used jet-A fuel with LOX and produced approximately 340 kN of thrust. The Merlin engine family would evolve through 1B, 1C, and 1D variants to become the backbone of Falcon 9.

events.title: NASA Awards COTS Space Act Agreement — $278M
events.detail: NASA awarded SpaceX a Commercial Orbital Transportation Services (COTS) Space Act Agreement worth up to $278M to develop Falcon 9 and demonstrate ISS cargo delivery. This was the first major government validation of SpaceX and effectively funded the development of Falcon 9 and Dragon spacecraft, transforming SpaceX from a small startup into a serious launch provider.

events.title: Falcon 1 Flight 1 — Explosion at T+25s
events.detail: The first Falcon 1 launch from Kwajalein Atoll ended 25 seconds after liftoff due to a fuel leak and engine fire in an A-nut on the fuel feed line. Despite the failure, SpaceX had demonstrated the first launch of a privately developed liquid-fueled rocket from a private launch facility.

events.title: Falcon 1 Flight 2 — Stage Separation Collision
events.detail: Falcon 1's second launch failed due to an interstage collision at stage separation — the first stage's residual thrust caused re-contact with the departing second stage. The vehicle carried a DARPA test payload and FalconSAT-2 for the US Air Force Academy. The team identified the fix: wait 1.5 seconds between cutoff and separation.

events.title: Falcon 1 Flight 3 — Same Staging Failure with Celebrity Ashes
events.detail: The third Falcon 1 launch carried three experimental satellites plus the cremated remains of Star Trek actor James Doohan and Mercury astronaut Gordon Cooper. The same separation issue recurred: residual first-stage thrust caused a collision. SpaceX was near bankruptcy with Musk dividing his last $75M between SpaceX and Tesla.

events.title: Falcon 1 Flight 4 — First Private Orbital Rocket Success
events.detail: Assembled in six weeks from salvaged hardware, Falcon 1's fourth launch reached orbit successfully. SpaceX became the first privately funded company to reach orbit with a liquid-fueled rocket. The success came just as Musk had run out of personal funding — NASA's CRS contract offer came three months later and saved the company.

events.title: NASA Awards $1.6B Commercial Resupply Contract
events.detail: Three months after Falcon 1's first orbital success, NASA awarded SpaceX a $1.6B Commercial Resupply Services (CRS) contract for a minimum of 12 ISS cargo missions. The contract was a turning point for SpaceX, providing steady revenue to fund Falcon 9 and Dragon development when private investment was scarce.

events.title: Falcon 1 Flight 5 — First Commercial Customer Launch
events.detail: Falcon 1's fifth and final launch successfully delivered Malaysia's RazakSAT Earth observation satellite to near-equatorial LEO. This was SpaceX's first commercial paying customer launch. Falcon 1 was subsequently retired in favor of the larger Falcon 9, with lessons from four failures and one success shaping the new vehicle's design.

events.title: Falcon 9 Design Finalized — Nine-Engine Cluster Chosen
events.detail: SpaceX finalized Falcon 9's design around nine Merlin engines on the first stage, providing engine-out capability that allowed the vehicle to complete missions even if one of nine engines failed. This reliability architecture, combined with the decision to use the same Merlin engine on both stages (for commonality), shaped Falcon 9 into the world's most successful orbital rocket.

events.title: Falcon 9 Makes Its Debut from SLC-40
events.detail: The first Falcon 9 launch from SLC-40, Cape Canaveral, successfully placed a Dragon qualification unit into LEO. The two-stage rocket powered by nine Merlin 1C engines at liftoff demonstrated SpaceX's new workhorse. Designed with nine Merlin engines to enable engine-out capability — if one fails, the mission can still succeed.

events.title: Dragon COTS Demo Flight 1 — First Orbital Commercial Recovery
events.detail: SpaceX launched Dragon C1 on Falcon 9's second flight. Dragon completed two Earth orbits before a successful Pacific splashdown, becoming the first commercially built and operated spacecraft recovered from orbit — a feat previously achieved only by NASA, the Soviet Union, and China.

events.title: Dragon Docks with ISS — First Commercial Spacecraft
events.detail: Dragon C2+ launched on COTS Demo Flight 2 and on May 25 berthed with the ISS via Canadarm2 — the first commercial spacecraft to achieve this milestone and the first American vehicle at ISS since Space Shuttle retirement. The crew offloaded 520 kg of cargo and returned 660 kg of scientific samples. Splashdown May 31.

events.title: CRS-1 — First Operational ISS Cargo Mission
events.detail: SpaceX launched the first operational Commercial Resupply Services mission, delivering 525 kg of pressurized cargo. One Merlin engine shut down at T+79s (engine-out), but Falcon 9 successfully reached orbit. This formally began the $1.6B CRS-1 contract execution and established Dragon as the ISS resupply workhorse.

events.title: NASA Awards $440M Commercial Crew CCiCap
events.detail: NASA selected SpaceX for a $440M Commercial Crew Integrated Capability (CCiCap) agreement, funding Crew Dragon development. This milestone, combined with Boeing receiving $460M, established the competitive commercial crew program intended to end US reliance on Russian Soyuz for astronaut transport at $80M per seat.

events.title: CRS-2 — Dragon Battles Thruster Failure to Reach ISS
events.detail: CRS-2 launched but Dragon experienced thruster pod failures after solar panel deployment, preventing ISS rendezvous for over a day. SpaceX engineers worked overnight to clear blocked valves, and Dragon successfully docked. The mission demonstrated Dragon's resilience and SpaceX's rapid problem-solving under pressure.

events.title: CRS-3 — First Dragon with Landing Leg Test Hardware
events.detail: CRS-3 successfully delivered cargo to the ISS and marked the first Falcon 9 flight with deployable landing legs on the first stage. The booster performed a controlled water landing in the Atlantic — not a proper landing, but a test of leg deployment and hover capability. SpaceX was openly preparing for full booster recovery.

events.title: ORBCOMM OG2 — 11 Satellites on a Single Falcon 9
events.detail: The ORBCOMM OG2 mission simultaneously delivered 11 commercial IoT satellites to orbit and was the recovery mission that yielded the first successful vertical booster landing. SpaceX demonstrated payload performance was not compromised by the landing-capable configuration, validating the commercial viability of the reuse approach.

events.title: Grasshopper VTVL Testbed Makes First Hop
events.detail: SpaceX's Grasshopper vertical-takeoff-vertical-landing testbed made its first 1.8-meter hop at McGregor, Texas. The 106-foot tall prototype — built on a Falcon 9 v1.0 first stage with landing legs — was the key development tool for the propulsive booster landing technology that would transform launch economics.

events.title: Grasshopper Reaches 744m Altitude — Program Complete
events.detail: Grasshopper's eighth and final test reached 744 meters (2,441 feet) before returning to land precisely on the pad. The program progressed from 1.8m hops to over 700m in just over a year, demonstrating viable propulsive landing technology. Grasshopper was retired and replaced by the higher-altitude F9R Dev program.

events.title: NASA Awards SpaceX $2.6B Commercial Crew Contract
events.detail: NASA awarded SpaceX a Commercial Crew Transportation Capability (CCtCap) fixed-price contract worth up to $2.6B (Boeing received $4.2B) to develop Crew Dragon. The contract required SpaceX to conduct an uncrewed Demo-1 and crewed Demo-2 before operational rotation missions. It targeted initial service by 2017.

events.title: CRS-4 — First Controlled Booster Ocean Soft-Landing
events.detail: During CRS-4, SpaceX attempted the first-ever controlled ocean soft landing of a Falcon 9 booster. The booster successfully performed reentry and landing burns, achieving a controlled soft splashdown in the Atlantic Ocean — the first time any orbital rocket had made a controlled descent and soft landing after payload delivery.

sources.name: SpaceX reusable launch system development — Wikipedia

events.title: CRS-5 — First Drone Ship Landing Attempt (Hard Landing)
events.detail: During CRS-5, SpaceX made its first attempt to land a Falcon 9 booster on drone ship 'Just Read the Instructions.' The booster found the droneship and performed a precision landing burn but ran out of hydraulic fluid for grid fin control, tipping over on contact. SpaceX called it 'close, but no cigar.'

events.title: CRS-6 — Second Drone Ship Attempt, Throttle Valve Issue
events.detail: CRS-6 made a second droneship landing attempt. The booster reached the drone ship but landed with too much lateral velocity and tipped over. A throttle valve stuck momentarily, preventing precise speed control in the final seconds. SpaceX noted the systems were getting closer to nominal with each test.

events.title: CRS-7 In-Flight Explosion — Dragon and Cargo Lost
events.detail: A catastrophic mission failure destroyed Dragon and 2,400 kg of ISS cargo 139 seconds after liftoff. An industrial-grade strut holding a high-pressure helium bottle in the second stage failed under load. The accident grounded SpaceX for six months and prompted comprehensive review of supply chain quality control procedures.

events.title: F9R Dev Program — Successor to Grasshopper at McGregor
events.detail: The F9R Dev 1 vehicle (an upgraded single-engine testbed) and F9R Dev 2 at Vandenberg extended Grasshopper's work, testing landing legs, grid fin steering, and higher-altitude hops. F9R Dev 2 reached ~1,000 meters before an anomaly triggered automatic destruct. These tests directly informed Falcon 9's first stage landing algorithm.

events.title: Historic First Orbital Booster Landing at LZ-1
events.detail: After its six-month post-CRS-7 stand-down, SpaceX returned to flight with the first successful vertical landing of an orbital-class rocket booster after mission completion. After delivering 11 ORBCOMM OG2 satellites to LEO, the Falcon 9 booster touched down at Landing Zone 1, Cape Canaveral — a milestone widely considered impossible by traditional aerospace.

events.title: SES-9 — Near-Miss Drone Ship Landing After GTO Mission
events.detail: Launching SES-9 to geostationary transfer orbit (the most challenging mission profile for recovery), the booster hit the drone ship 'Of Course I Still Love You' but at excessive speed and tipped over. Still, SpaceX had now consistently reached the drone ship — the precision was improving with every attempt.

events.title: CRS-8 — First Successful Drone Ship Landing
events.detail: The CRS-8 mission marked the first successful landing of a Falcon 9 booster at sea. After delivering Dragon to orbit, booster B1021 landed cleanly on 'Of Course I Still Love You' in the Atlantic. This proved downrange drone ship landings — essential for high-energy missions — were operationally viable.

events.title: AMOS-6 Pad Explosion — SLC-40 Destroyed
events.detail: During a routine pre-launch static fire at SLC-40, the Falcon 9 second stage exploded in 93 milliseconds, destroying the vehicle, the $300M AMOS-6 satellite, and severely damaging SLC-40. Investigation found a COPV helium pressure vessel failure. SpaceX was grounded four months and had to conduct all missions from LC-39A during SLC-40's year-long reconstruction.

events.title: Return to Flight — Iridium-1 Launches 10 Satellites
events.detail: SpaceX returned to flight with the Iridium-1 mission from Vandenberg, successfully launching 10 Iridium NEXT satellites and landing the booster on 'Just Read the Instructions.' The four-month stand-down had allowed SpaceX to redesign COPV helium tanks and rebuild SLC-40, which returned to service in December 2017.

events.title: Jason-3 — Second Drone Ship Attempt, Leg Collapses on Landing
events.detail: The Jason-3 oceanography satellite mission produced the most dramatic near-miss footage yet: the booster landed perfectly on the drone ship, but one of four legs failed to latch, causing the vehicle to slowly topple and explode. SpaceX noted the landing itself was nominal — only a locking collet failure prevented a full success.

events.title: SES-10 — First Orbital Booster Reflown
events.detail: Booster B1021 — previously flown on CRS-8 — launched SES-10 and successfully landed again on 'Of Course I Still Love You.' This was the first time an orbital-class rocket booster had been reflown, validating the entire SpaceX reusability business model. Musk called it 'an amazing, incredible day' — the threshold between a rocket company and a revolution.

events.title: NROL-76 — First Classified DoD Mission for SpaceX
events.detail: SpaceX launched the classified NROL-76 satellite for the National Reconnaissance Office — its first mission for the US intelligence community. The mission demonstrated SpaceX's ability to handle classified payloads, opening up a major market previously dominated exclusively by ULA with its Atlas V and Delta IV vehicles.

events.title: Starlink Prototype Satellites (Tintin A & B) Launched
events.detail: SpaceX launched the first two Starlink technology demonstrators, dubbed Tintin A and B, on a Falcon 9 from Vandenberg. These prototypes tested phased-array antennas, optical inter-satellite links, and krypton-fueled Hall-effect thrusters — the key technologies for the eventual mega-constellation.

events.title: Falcon Heavy Test Flight — Starman Goes to Space
events.detail: Falcon Heavy, the most powerful rocket then in operation at 5 million lbf thrust, debuted from LC-39A. Payload was Elon Musk's personal Tesla Roadster with dummy 'Starman' sent toward a heliocentric orbit. The two side boosters executed a stunning simultaneous landing at LZ-1 and LZ-2. The center core missed the drone ship at speed.

events.title: Falcon 9 Block 5 — Definitive Human-Rated Variant Debuts
events.detail: The final Falcon 9 Block 5 variant debuted with the Bangabandhu-1 satellite. Block 5 features upgraded Merlin 1D+ engines, thermal protection for rapid reuse, titanium grid fins for improved aerodynamic control, and landing legs redesigned for fast refurbishment. SpaceX certified Block 5 for NASA human spaceflight.

events.title: SpaceX Becomes World's Most Prolific Launch Provider
events.detail: By end of 2018, SpaceX had surpassed all other operators to become the world's most prolific launch provider by mass to orbit. The company launched 21 times in 2018, delivering over 65,000 kg to orbit, capturing roughly 60% of global commercial launch market share — a transformation achieved in just 8 years since Falcon 9's debut.

events.title: Crew Dragon In-Flight Abort Test at Max-Q
events.detail: SpaceX conducted a full in-flight abort test, triggering the SuperDraco escape system at maximum aerodynamic pressure. The Crew Dragon capsule successfully escaped a deliberately terminated Falcon 9 at ~1.3 km altitude, validating the launch escape system required for NASA human rating certification.

sources.name: Crew Dragon In-Flight Abort Test — Wikipedia

events.title: BulgariaSat-1 — First Reflown Booster on GTO Mission
events.detail: SpaceX reflew booster B1029 (previously flown on CRS-10) on the heavy GTO mission BulgariaSat-1, demonstrating that reused boosters could perform demanding geostationary transfer orbit insertions. The booster landed on 'Of Course I Still Love You' after the high-energy burn. SpaceX was charging customers ~10% discount for reused boosters.

events.title: Falcon Heavy Arabsat-6A — First Commercial FH Mission
events.detail: Falcon Heavy's first commercial mission (after the 2018 test flight) launched Arabsat-6A to geostationary orbit. All three boosters landed successfully — both side boosters at LZ-1 and LZ-2 simultaneously, and the center core on 'Of Course I Still Love You' — demonstrating full operational reusability of the world's most powerful operational rocket.

events.title: Crew Dragon Demo-1 — First Autonomous ISS Docking
events.detail: The uncrewed Crew Dragon 'Endeavour' launched from LC-39A and autonomously docked with the ISS on March 3 — the first commercial spacecraft to achieve fully autonomous rendezvous and docking. Carrying mannequin 'Ripley' with instrumentation to measure forces on a crewed occupant, it splashdown March 8.

events.title: Demo-1 Capsule Destroyed in SuperDraco Test Anomaly
events.detail: The Demo-1 Crew Dragon capsule was destroyed during a SuperDraco abort engine test on the pad. A non-return check valve allowed nitrogen tetroxide oxidizer to enter high-pressure helium lines, triggering an explosion. SpaceX redesigned the problematic valve system before proceeding with Demo-2.

events.title: Starlink Operational Constellation Begins — 60 Satellites
events.detail: SpaceX launched 60 Block v0.9 Starlink satellites on a twice-flown Falcon 9 from SLC-40, initiating the operational constellation. Within weeks, astronomers raised alarms about satellite streaks in telescope images. SpaceX committed to developing sunshades and darkening measures to reduce optical brightness.

events.title: Demo-2 — First Crewed US Orbital Launch Since Shuttle
events.detail: NASA astronauts Bob Behnken and Doug Hurley launched to ISS aboard Crew Dragon 'Endeavour,' the first crewed orbital launch from US soil since Space Shuttle Atlantis in July 2011. The 62-day mission ended with a Gulf of Mexico splashdown on August 2 — the first US crew water landing since Apollo-Soyuz in 1975. This earned Crew Dragon its NASA human-rating certification.

events.title: Starship SN5 — First Full-Scale 150m Hop
events.detail: Starship prototype SN5 completed a 150-meter hop at Boca Chica, Texas — the first flight of a full-scale Starship propellant tank section using a single Raptor engine. The vehicle landed intact. Musk tweeted: 'Mars is looking real.' It was a dramatic leap from the smaller Starhopper tests of 2019.

events.title: Starlink Surpasses 180 Satellites — Beta Testing Begins
events.detail: The Starlink constellation grew to 180+ satellites through rapid monthly launches, and SpaceX opened a limited private beta for employee testing. The 'Better Than Nothing Beta' later opened to public users in November 2020 with speeds of 50-150 Mbps and latency under 20ms — far better than legacy satellite internet services.

events.title: Starship SN6 — Second Successful 150m Hop
events.detail: Starship SN6 successfully completed its own 150-meter hop at Boca Chica, following SN5's success the prior month. The back-to-back hops confirmed the single-Raptor test configuration was reliable and allowed SpaceX to begin preparing three-engine vehicles for the high-altitude program.

events.title: Crew-1 — First Operational Commercial Crew Rotation
events.detail: NASA's first operational Commercial Crew rotation mission launched four astronauts: Victor Glover, Mike Hopkins, Shannon Walker (NASA), and Soichi Noguchi (JAXA). Crew Dragon 'Resilience' docked with ISS for a six-month increment, establishing the standard US ISS crew rotation model and ending reliance on Russian Soyuz.

events.title: Starship SN8 — 12.5km Belly Flop Test
events.detail: Starship SN8 reached 12.5 km altitude in a three-Raptor flight, performing the 'belly flop' aerodynamic reentry maneuver for the first time on a full-scale vehicle before exploding on landing due to low header tank pressure. Despite the fiery end, the controlled belly-flop maneuver was the key demonstration SpaceX needed.

events.title: Starship SN10 — First Intact Landing (Explodes 8 Min Later)
events.detail: SN10 became the first full-scale Starship to land intact after a 10 km flight. However, approximately 8 minutes post-landing the vehicle exploded due to a propellant leak. SpaceX engineers immediately identified the fix for subsequent vehicles. The landing itself was celebrated as a milestone.

events.title: Starship SN15 — First Clean 10km Landing
events.detail: Starship SN15 completed a full 10 km test flight and landed cleanly at Boca Chica — the first time a full-scale Starship prototype had landed without exploding. This validated the belly-flop maneuver, heat shield performance at test altitudes, and landing leg deployment. SN15 remained intact and was later decommissioned as a historic artifact.

events.title: NASA Selects SpaceX Starship as Artemis Lunar Lander
events.detail: NASA awarded SpaceX a $2.89B Human Landing System contract for a Starship-based lunar lander for the Artemis program. The sole-source selection over Blue Origin and Dynetics prompted Blue Origin's protest, a 7-month program suspension, and ultimately Congressional pressure to fund a second provider.

events.title: Inspiration4 — First All-Private Orbital Crew
events.detail: SpaceX launched Inspiration4, the first fully private orbital crew mission, with four non-government astronauts spending three days in a 585 km orbit — higher than the ISS. Commander Jared Isaacman organized the flight partly as a fundraiser for St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, raising over $200M. The crew even had a transparent observation dome replacing the docking port.

events.title: Axiom-1 — First Fully Private Astronaut ISS Mission
events.detail: SpaceX launched Axiom Space's first mission with four private astronauts including former NASA astronaut Michael López-Alegría. Ax-1 demonstrated the commercial viability of Crew Dragon for non-government customers and opened a new business line for SpaceX beyond NASA contracts.

events.title: SpaceX Conducts 61 Orbital Launches — Annual Record
events.detail: In 2022, SpaceX conducted 61 orbital launches — more than any single country or company had ever achieved in a calendar year. With Starlink dominating the manifest, SpaceX launched more mass to orbit than all other global launch providers combined, a dominance sustained by Falcon 9's rapid turnaround cadence with reused boosters.

events.title: SN9 Launch Prompts FAA Enforcement Investigation
events.detail: SpaceX launched Starship SN9 before receiving final FAA approval, prompting the FAA to open a safety investigation. The agency temporarily grounded SpaceX's Starship program until a corrective action plan was accepted. The episode highlighted a regulatory tension that would recur: SpaceX's iterative-testing pace versus the FAA's licensing timelines.

events.title: Raptor Vacuum Engine Completes Full-Duration Test
events.detail: SpaceX completed full-duration test fires of the Raptor Vacuum (RVac) engine at McGregor — the nozzle-extended variant for Starship's upper stage. With a vacuum specific impulse exceeding 380s and 200+ tf thrust, the RVac gives Starship exceptional upper-stage performance for deep-space missions and payload delivery.

events.title: IFT-1 — Starship Lifts Off; Lost at T+4 Min
events.detail: The first Starship/Super Heavy integrated flight test launched from Boca Chica, briefly making it the most powerful rocket ever flown at 74 MN thrust. Multiple engines failed before launch; the vehicle tumbled and was destroyed by the flight termination system approximately 4 minutes after launch. The launch pad sustained significant damage, delaying the next test.

events.title: Steel Plate Water Deluge System Built Under Launch Mount
events.detail: Following IFT-1's launch pad damage, SpaceX rapidly constructed a massive steel plate water deluge system beneath the orbital launch mount. The system, tested with massive water flows, dramatically reduced pad damage in subsequent Starship tests, enabling faster turnaround between integrated flight tests.

events.title: IFT-2 — First Starship to Reach Space; Hot-Staging Debut
events.detail: IFT-2 introduced hot-staging (firing Ship engines while Super Heavy is still attached) and saw Ship 25 become the first Starship to reach space. Super Heavy was intentionally terminated over the Gulf of Mexico after hot-staging, while Ship 25 flew for over eight minutes before being destroyed during reentry by its automated flight termination system.

events.title: IFT-3 — Full Second-Stage Burn and Near-Orbital Velocity
events.detail: IFT-3 achieved the first full-duration upper stage burn and near-orbital velocity, with Ship 28 surviving reentry and performing the flip maneuver before breaking apart at ~65 km altitude over the Indian Ocean. Super Heavy performed a controlled splashdown in the Gulf. Tile loss on reentry and fin damage provided key engineering data.

events.title: IFT-4 — Controlled Splashdowns for Both Vehicles
events.detail: IFT-4 achieved controlled splashdowns for both Super Heavy (Gulf of Mexico) and Ship (Indian Ocean). Both vehicles survived reentry with controlled descent. This validated the heat shield and reentry profiles needed for booster catch and eventual orbital operations, earning praise from NASA's Artemis program managers.

events.title: IFT-5 — Super Heavy Caught by Mechazilla Arms
events.detail: In perhaps the most dramatic engineering achievement since the first Falcon 9 landing, IFT-5 saw the Super Heavy booster caught mid-air by the mechanical 'chopstick' arms of the Starbase launch tower — the first time any full-scale rocket booster had been caught. Ship 30 simultaneously performed a precision ocean splashdown near Kauai.

events.title: IFT-6 — Second Booster Catch and Improved Ship Splashdown
events.detail: The sixth Starship integrated flight test caught Super Heavy again with the Mechazilla arms and achieved a more controlled Ship splashdown in the Indian Ocean. SpaceX accumulated critical data on heat shield tile performance, fuel cell power systems, and the precision of the landing flip maneuver for future operational missions.

events.title: Polaris Dawn — First Commercial Spacewalk in History
events.detail: The Polaris Dawn mission launched four private astronauts to 1,400 km (highest orbit since Apollo), then descended to 700 km for the first commercial spacewalk on September 12. Commander Jared Isaacman and SpaceX engineer Sarah Gillis conducted the EVA in SpaceX-designed suits, demonstrating commercial extravehicular activity capability.

events.title: Starlink Surpasses 6,000 Active Satellites
events.detail: By 2024, the Starlink constellation exceeded 6,000 active satellites with Gen2 satellites featuring higher throughput and direct-to-cell (D2C) capability. SpaceX's broadband service reached over 4 million subscribers globally, with contracts signed with national governments, airlines, maritime operators, and military users worldwide.

events.title: SpaceX Conducts 96 Launches in 2023 — New Annual Record
events.detail: SpaceX conducted 96 orbital launches in 2023, again setting a new global annual record and representing roughly half of all worldwide orbital launches. Starlink dominated the manifest, but commercial, civil, and national security payloads filled the remainder. SpaceX's share of global launch mass exceeded 80% for the year.

events.title: Starlink Deployed in Ukraine After Russian Invasion
events.detail: Within days of Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, SpaceX activated Starlink service in Ukraine and shipped thousands of terminals at Musk's direction. Starlink provided critical battlefield connectivity to Ukrainian forces and civilians when Russian attacks disrupted infrastructure. The deployment became the most prominent military use of a commercial satellite constellation in history.

events.title: Raptor 3 Engine Demonstrated at Record Thrust
events.detail: SpaceX demonstrated the Raptor 3 engine at McGregor, achieving over 280 tf (2.75 MN) of thrust at full power — a significant improvement over the original Raptor 1's ~185 tf. The Raptor 3's simplified design (fewer components, no external plumbing) also improved reliability and manufacturing speed, essential for Starship's planned rapid-reuse operations.

events.title: Starshield — SpaceX Wins $70M DoD Classified Satellite Contract
events.detail: The US Space Force awarded SpaceX a contract for Starshield, a classified military variant of Starlink optimized for national security applications with government-controlled encryption and higher security protocols. Starshield represented SpaceX's formal entry as a national security space prime contractor alongside traditional players like Northrop Grumman and Boeing.

events.title: IFT-7 — Third Consecutive Booster Catch
events.detail: The seventh Starship integrated flight test achieved a third consecutive Super Heavy booster catch by the Mechazilla arms at Starbase. Ship 34 performed the belly-flop maneuver and completed a precision ocean splashdown. SpaceX announced plans to attempt the first Ship catch at the launch tower in the next flight test.

events.title: Starbase Becomes an Official City of Texas
events.detail: Voters in the Boca Chica area approved incorporation, creating the City of Starbase, Texas. The new municipality encompasses SpaceX's South Texas launch facility and its growing workforce community. The incorporation resolved longstanding land-use tensions with Cameron County and gave SpaceX clearer local governance authority.

events.title: Crew Dragon Returns Starliner Astronauts After Nine-Month Delay
events.detail: NASA astronauts Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams, who had launched aboard Boeing Starliner in June 2024 for an eight-day mission, returned to Earth aboard a Crew Dragon in March 2025 after NASA deemed Starliner too risky for crewed reentry. SpaceX stepped in to provide the rescue mission, further cementing Crew Dragon's role as NASA's primary crewed vehicle.

sources.name: Boeing Starliner Butch and Suni — Reuters

events.title: Starlink Direct-to-Cell Service Launches Commercially
events.detail: SpaceX's Starlink Direct-to-Cell service launched commercially in the United States, enabling standard mobile phones to connect directly to Starlink satellites without additional hardware. T-Mobile and other carrier partnerships brought the service to millions of users in remote areas, fulfilling a long-promised capability for universal mobile coverage.

events.title: Falcon 9 Booster Surpasses 25 Individual Flights
events.detail: Multiple Falcon 9 Block 5 boosters surpassed 20 flights, with leading boosters reaching 25+ reflights by 2025. Some boosters turned around in under 24 hours between missions. The rapid reuse cadence enabled SpaceX to conduct over 60 launches per year with a relatively small fleet of hardware.

events.title: SpaceX Approaches 24-Year Anniversary — Mars Timeline Updates
events.detail: Approaching its 24th anniversary, SpaceX operates the world's largest commercial launch system, the world's largest satellite constellation at 10,000+ active Starlinks serving 10 million users, and the most powerful rocket ever built in Starship. The company's valuation exceeds $350B and Musk has maintained a crewed Mars mission target of the late 2020s.
